title: Excitonic-Superconducting Coexistence and Emergent Nematic Superconductivity Driven by Spontaneous Symmetry Breaking
authors: Fei Yang, Ruigang Li, Junwei Liu, Binghai Yan
abstract: Excitonic insulating (EI) and superconducting (SC) orders are generally regarded as mutually exclusive electronic instabilities. Within a self-consistent microscopic theory, we study electronic systems hosting an EI phase in the presence of SC pairing and show that an intrinsic mismatch between electron and hole Fermi surfaces fundamentally reshapes this competition. This mismatch stabilizes FFLO-like electron-hole pairing and drives spontaneous symmetry breaking of the EI state. The resulting symmetry breaking reconstructs the pairing phase space for SC and EI state, such that different regions of the Fermi surface complementarily support either EI or SC correlations, leading to a natural coexistence of the two orders. Notably, the emergent SC state consequently breaks rotational symmetry and develops intrinsic nematic superconductivity, even in the absence of explicit symmetry-breaking fields (such as magnetic fields, spin-orbit coupling, or bare band-structure anisotropy). Our results suggest that candidate materials such as monolayer 1T$'$-MoTe$_2$ and the square-net semimetal NaAlSi may provide promising platforms for observing this phenomenon. More broadly, these findings reveal a unique mechanism by which competing many-body orders generate electronic nematicity, suggesting a broader route toward spontaneous anisotropic electronic states in correlated quantum materials.
summary: This theoretical work demonstrates that the competition between excitonic and superconducting orders can lead to a novel coexistence regime driven by Fermi surface mismatch. This coexistence forces the superconducting state to inherit an anisotropic structure, resulting in intrinsic nematic superconductivity even without external symmetry-breaking fields. The findings suggest a general mechanism for generating electronic nematicity in correlated quantum materials.
main_problem: The paper investigates the competition and coexistence between Excitonic Insulating (EI) and Superconducting (SC) orders, which are generally considered mutually exclusive instabilities.
main_result: The intrinsic mismatch between electron and hole Fermi surfaces stabilizes a finite-momentum excitonic order, which reconstructs the pairing phase space to allow for the coexistence of EI and SC states, leading to emergent nematic superconductivity.
method: The study employs a self-consistent mean-field theory, solving coupled gap equations for both SC and EI order parameters.
model_or_system: The model considers correlated quantum materials, using a two-band Hamiltonian approach that incorporates both superconducting pairing and interband Coulomb interactions driving the excitonic instability.
key_observables: Superfluid density tensor components ($ho_{s,xx}, ho_{s,yy}$), EI gap ($\Delta_{EI}$), SC gap ($\Delta_{SC}$), and the excitonic ordering wave vector ($\mathbf{q}$).
important_parameters: Carrier density ($n$), temperature ($T$), and the degree of Fermi surface mismatch.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on mean-field approximations and often assumes an isotropic parabolic band structure for initial simplicity, though extensions consider intervalley effects.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the schematic pairing on mismatched Fermi surfaces, the momentum-resolved SC correlation, and the superfluid density tensor components showing anisotropy ($ho_{s,xx} > ho_{s,yy}$) in the coexistence regime.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by establishing the theoretical framework (Hamiltonian, gap equations), analyzing the role of Fermi surface mismatch in stabilizing finite-momentum excitonic order, demonstrating the resulting phase space reconstruction, and finally calculating the anisotropic superfluid response as the signature of nematic coexistence.
why_interesting: The mechanism of generating intrinsic nematicity purely from the competition between two competing many-body orders (EI and SC) is a general concept applicable to understanding anisotropic electronic states in correlated materials, which is highly relevant to modern condensed matter theory.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Bare band structure showing the conduction and valence bands with a negative band gap 𝐸𝑔. (b) Schematic illustration of SC and EI pairing on mismatched Fermi surfaces. The purple dashed lines denote interband electron-hole EI pairings. Due to the electron- hole Fermi-surface mismatch, the EI pairing spontaneously acquires finite CM momenta ±q, forming an LO-like excitonic state composed of two time-reversal-related pairing components. Here, q is chosen along the 𝑦direction, reflecting the spontaneous rotational-symmetry breaking of the underlying isotropic model. As a result, conduction- band states with momenta perpendicular to ±q do not participate in EI pairing, leaving parts...

FIG. 2. (a) Carrier-density dependence of the EI gap, SC gap, and ordering wave vector magnitude 𝑞for 𝐸𝑔= 100 meV. Here, 𝑛= 𝑛𝑐−𝑛𝑣denotes the net carrier density and 𝑞0 = |𝑘𝐹,𝑣−𝑘𝐹,𝑐|. Inset: corresponding results for 𝐸𝑔= 50 meV and 20 meV. (b)- (d) and (e)-(g) show the momentum-resolved EI and SC correlations for conduction-band electrons within a momentum shell around the Fermi surface, respectively, at three representative electron-doped carrier densities corresponding to the EI-dominated, coexistence, and SC-dominated regimes when 𝐸𝑔= 100 meV.

FIG. 3. Superfluid-density tensor components 𝜌𝑠,𝑥𝑥and 𝜌𝑠,𝑦𝑦, and the nematic component 𝛿𝜌𝑠= 𝜌𝑠,𝑥𝑥−𝜌𝑠,𝑦𝑦, versus net carrier density 𝑛= 𝑛𝑐−𝑛𝑣. Insets show the angular dependence of 𝜌𝑠(𝜃) in the EI- dominated, coexistence, and SC-dominated regimes on both the hole- and electron-doped sides. Here, 𝑛0 = 1012 cm−2. The dashed line denotes the conduction-band density 𝑛𝑐, where superconductivity resides; thus, in the SC-dominated regime (|𝑛| > 14𝑛0), 𝜌𝑠,𝑥𝑥≈ 𝜌𝑠,𝑦𝑦≈𝑛𝑐. The phase regimes are determined from Fig. 2(a).
